10: lo yasur shevet miYehudah umechokek mibein raglav ad ki yavo shiloh velo yiqqehat amim: 'The scepter shall not depart from Judah, nor the ruler's staff from between his feet, until tribute comes to him; and to him shall be the obedience of the peoples.' Shiloh: the most debated word in Jacob's blessing. Major interpretations: (1) a place name (Shiloh in Ephraim — problematic geographically); (2) a form of sheloh = 'whose it is' (Ezekiel 21:27 uses similar language); (3) a Messianic title derived from shalom (peace). The LXX reads 'until what is reserved for him comes' — pointing toward a coming ruler. Christian and early Jewish messianic readings consistently identified this as a promise of a coming king who would gather the nations.
1: bĕʾaḥărît hayyāmîm — "in the latter days" (H319 ʾaḥărît + H3117 yôm). ʾAḥărît hayyāmîm is the eschatological formula that introduces prophetic vision throughout the canonical prophets: Isaiah 2:2, Micah 4:1, Hosea 3:5, Daniel 10:14. Here it frames the tribal blessings as prophetic-historical vision, not merely patriarchal testimony — Jacob sees the destiny of the tribes across time. The phrase carries forward into the NT as "the last days" (Hebrews 1:2; Acts 2:17), establishing the typological horizon of the passage.
2: hāʾăzînû... šimʿû — "give ear… listen" — the dual hearing-imperative that opens formal poetry and prophetic address (cf. Isaiah 1:2; Deuteronomy 32:1). The doubled imperative transitions the prose narrative into the elevated register of Hebrew poetry; Genesis 49 is the first sustained piece of tribal poetry in the Pentateuch. Hāʾăzên (H238, to give ear/hearken) is more formal than šāmaʿ (H8085, hear) — together they call for attentive, receptive listening to a word of weight.
3: rēʾšît ʾônî — "the firstfruits of my strength/vigor." ʾÔn (H205) carries a productive ambiguity: it means both vigor/virile strength (used positively in Deuteronomy 21:17 for the firstborn's "firstfruits of his strength") and iniquity/trouble/wickedness (used negatively in Psalms 66:18; Hosea 12:11). The double meaning shadows the verse: Jacob praises Reuben as the firstfruits of his vigor while the word carries the seed of its own negation — the trouble (ʾôn) Reuben brought by defiling his father's bed (v4; Genesis 35:22). Rēʾšît (H7225, firstfruits/beginning) — the same word as in Genesis 1:1 ("in the beginning").
4: paḥaz kammayim lōʾ tôtar — "turbulent as the waters, you will not excel." Paḥaz (H6349, recklessness/instability/wantonness) — a rare word appearing only here, Judges 9:4 ("reckless men"), and Jeremiah 23:32 ("reckless lying"). The water-simile: water that is paḥaz overflows its banks, cannot be contained; it cannot "excel" (tôtar, H3498, to remain/excel/have advantage). Reuben's character is the character of uncontrollable overflow. The sin named: kî ʿālîtā mišĕḵāḇ ʾāḇîḵā (you went up to your father's bed) — the Qal perfect with the ascending preposition ʿālāh applied to what should have been avoidance. The same sexual violation is recorded without comment in Genesis 35:22.
5: kĕlê ḥāmās mĕḵērōtêhem — "their swords are weapons of violence." Ḥāmās (H2555, violence/injustice/wrong) — the word used for the earth's violence that provoked the flood (Genesis 6:11,13); the violence of oppression and assault. The word mĕḵērōtêhem is debated: MT reads a rare noun sometimes understood as "dwelling/kinship" or possibly "swords/weapons." The phrase frames Simeon and Levi as a pair whose instrument is violence — pointing back to the Shechem massacre (Genesis 34:25-29), which Jacob explicitly recalls in v6.
6: bĕsōḏām ʾal-tāḇōʾ napšî — "let my soul not enter their council." Sôḏ (H5475, council/secret counsel/intimate circle) — the word for the intimate circle of the divine council (Psalms 25:14; Jeremiah 23:18; Amos 3:7: "God does nothing without revealing his sôḏ to his servants the prophets"). Jacob invokes it negatively: he wants his nepeš excluded from their deliberating-circle. Hōrĕgû ʾîš (they killed a man) and ʿiqqĕrû-šôr (they hamstrung oxen) recall the Shechem massacre; the violence extended from persons to property.
7: ʾarrûr ʾappām kî ʿāz wĕʿeḇrātām kî qāšātāh — "Cursed be their anger, so fierce, and their fury, so cruel!" The curse falls not on the men themselves but on their anger and fury — a precision in blessing/cursing that distinguishes disposition from person. ʾAppām (their anger, H639, literally "their nose/nostrils" — the nose flares with anger) + ʿeḇrāh (H5678, fury/overflow of anger — from ʿāḇar, to overflow). The sentence: ʾăḥalleqēm bĕyaʿăqōḇ wăʾăpîṣēm bĕyiśrāʾēl (I will divide them in Jacob and scatter them in Israel) — the future of Simeon (absorbed by Judah, lacking territory) and Levi (given scattered cities; Num 35) is traced to this moral pronouncement.
8: yĕhûḏāh ʾattāh yôḏûḵā ʾaḥeḵā — "Judah, your brothers will praise you." The wordplay governing the entire Judah-blessing: yĕhûḏāh (Judah) derives from yāḏāh (H3034, to praise/give thanks/confess) — Leah named him "Judah" because "this time I will praise YHWH" (Genesis 29:35). Here the name and function align: "Judah = praise; your brothers will praise (yôḏûḵā) you." Yāḏĕḵā bĕʿōrep ʾōyĕḇeḵā (your hand on the neck of your enemies) — the posture of victory over a prostrated enemy; ʿōrep (H6203, back of neck) as the place one's hand goes in triumph (Joshua 10:24). Yiššĕtaḥăwû lĕḵā bĕnê ʾāḇîḵā — "your father's sons will bow down to you" — the echo of Joseph's dream (Genesis 37:7-9) now repurposed for the dynastic future of Judah.
9: gûr ʾaryēh yĕhûḏāh — "Judah is a lion's cub." Three lion-words accumulate: gûr (H1482, young lion/cub — a lion in its prime developmental stage), ʾaryēh (H738, the adult male lion — the standard term), lābîʾ (H3833, a great lion/lioness — an archaic poetic term for the mightiest lion, used in Job 4:11; Isaiah 30:6). The triple progression — cub, lion, great lion — traces Judah's dynasty from its origin through its mature power to its supreme dominion. Mî yĕqîmennû (who will rouse him?) — the lion at rest challenges all comers; the rhetorical question implies "no one." Revelation 5:5 ("the Lion of the tribe of Judah has prevailed") picks up this exact imagery for Christ.
11: ʾōsrî laggepen ʿîrōh wĕlašśōrēqāh bĕnî ʾătōnô — "tying his donkey to a vine, his colt to the choice vine." The imagery is extravagance: in normal agriculture one does not tether donkeys to vines because the donkey would eat them. The prosperity of Judah's land is so extreme that vines are used as hitching-posts. Śōrēqāh (H8321, choice vine/red vine — a prized variety) — even the most valuable vine is expendable. Kibbēs bayyāyin lĕbûšô ûḇĕḏam-ʿănāḇîm sûtōh (washing his garments in wine and his robe in the blood of grapes) — wine used for laundry signals such abundance that the most costly liquid is used for the most mundane task. Revelation 19:13 ("a robe dipped in blood") echoes the wine-dipped garment imagery.
12: ḥaḵlĭlî ʿênayim miyyāyin ûlĕḇen-šinnayim mēḥālāḇ — "his eyes are darker than wine, his teeth whiter than milk." The verse describes the physical effects of abundance on Judah's people. Ḥaklĭlî (H2447, dark/sparkling/reddened — hapax; possibly related to darkening from wine saturation) modifies ʿênayim (eyes). The comparative prepositions min (than) structure the verse as a prosperity-marker: darker-than-wine eyes and whiter-than-milk teeth are the hallmarks of the well-fed inhabitant of Judah's territory.
13: zĕḇûlun lĕḥôp yammîm yiškōn wĕhûʾ lĕḥôp ʾŏniyyôt — "Zebulun will live at the seashore and become a haven for ships." The tribal territory of Zebulun in the historical settlement is actually inland in lower Galilee (Joshua 19:10-16), not coastal — which has led to various attempts to reconcile geography with this prophecy. The most common reading: Zebulun controlled access to shipping routes through the Jezreel Valley to the Mediterranean. Wĕḵanʿan ʿad-ṣîḏōn (toward Sidon) — Sidon was the great Phoenician coastal city; Zebulun's border extending toward it confirms maritime-trade association.
14: yiśśāḵār ḥămōr gārem rōḇēṣ bên hammišpĕtāyim — "Issachar is a rawboned (gārem) donkey lying between two saddlebags." Ḥămōr gārem (H2543 + H1634, a bony/rawboned donkey — a large, strong-framed pack animal) — not a negative image; a rawboned donkey is the strongest load-bearer. Gārem (H1634, bone/bony) — the emphasis on bone suggests structural strength rather than emaciation. Rōḇēṣ bên hammišpĕtāyim (lying between the two packs/saddlebags) — the pack animal at rest between its burdens, content with its position. The image sets up v15: Issachar accepts a burden-bearing role in exchange for pleasant land.
15: wayyarʾ mĕnûḥāh kî ṭôḇ wĕʾet-hāʾāreṣ kî nāʿēmāh wayyēṭ šikmô liskōl wĕhāyāh lĕmas ʿōḇēd — "When he sees that his resting place is good and that the land is pleasant, he will bend his shoulder to the burden and submit to forced labor." Wayyēṭ šikmô liskōl (he bent his shoulder to carry) — šeḵem (H7926, shoulder/back — the body part that bears burdens) + sāḵal (H5445, to carry/bear a heavy load). The moral evaluation is mixed: Issachar gains pleasant land but at the price of servile submission (mas ʿōḇēd, H4522 + H5647, tribute/forced labor + to serve). The tribe that should have ruled trades freedom for comfort.
16: dān yāḏîn ʿammô kĕʾaḥaḏ šiḇṭê yiśrāʾēl — "Dan will provide justice for his people as one of the tribes of Israel." The foundational wordplay: dān (Dan) from dîn (H1777, to judge/rule/govern) — Rachel named him Dan because "God has judged (dānannî) me" (Genesis 30:6). The blessing confirms: Dan will govern/judge (yāḏîn) his people. Kĕʾaḥaḏ šiḇṭê yiśrāʾēl (as one of the tribes of Israel) — the phrase ensures Dan's inclusion despite being the son of a concubine (Bilhah); his tribal status is equal to the sons of Leah and Rachel.
17: yĕhî ḏān nāḥāš ʿălê-dāreḵ šĕpîpōn ʿălê-ʾōraḥ — "Dan will be a serpent by the roadside, a viper along the path." Two snake-words: nāḥāš (H5175, the standard word for serpent — used for the Eden serpent in Genesis 3) + šĕpîpōn (H8207, a horned viper — a specific venomous snake, possibly Cerastes cornutus, which is small, camouflaged, and strikes suddenly). The guerrilla-warfare image: the hidden viper bites the horse's heels so the rider falls backward. hannōšēk ʿiqĕḇê-sûs wayyippōl rōḵĕḇô ʾāḥôr (biting the horse's heels so that its rider tumbles backward) — the heel-biting echoes the Eden curse (Genesis 3:15: the serpent strikes the heel); the serpent-heel pattern resonates across Genesis.
18: lîšûʿāṯĕḵā qiwwîtî YHWH — "For your salvation (yĕšûʿāh) I wait (qiwwîtî), O YHWH." A sudden liturgical interjection in the middle of the tribal blessings — the only direct-address prayer in Genesis 49. Qiwwāh (H6960, to wait/hope for/look for with expectation) — the primary prophetic waiting-verb: "those who wait for (qōwê) YHWH shall renew their strength" (Isaiah 40:31); "I wait for YHWH, my soul waits" (Psalms 130:5). The brevity is striking: three Hebrew words interrupting the tribal sequence. Jacob inserts his own prayer amid the dangerous imagery of Dan-the-serpent — as if seeing the nation's fragility amid its tribal arrangements and calling out for divine deliverance. Yĕšûʿāh (H3444, salvation/deliverance) — the root of the name Joshua/Jesus (yēšûaʿ).
19: gāḏ gĕḏûḏ yĕgûḏennû wĕhûʾ yāgûḏ ʿāqēḇ — "Gad — a raiding band will raid him, but he will raid their heel." The verse is a sustained sound-pun: gāḏ (Gad, H1410) + gĕḏûḏ (H1416, raiding band/troop) + yĕgûḏennû (they will raid him) + yāgûḏ (he will raid) — four occurrences of the same root gûḏ (H1464, to attack/raid) in four words. Leah named him Gad for "good fortune" (Genesis 30:11), but here the name is activated militarily: Gad's people will be raided and will counter-raid. ʿĀqēḇ (heel, H6119) — the third occurrence of the heel-image in Genesis (Genesis 3:15; Genesis 25:26; Genesis 49:17,19).
20: mēʾāšēr šmānāh laḥmô wĕhûʾ yittēn maʿăḏannê-melek — "Asher's food will be rich (šmānāh) and he will provide royal delicacies." Šāmēn (H8082, fat/rich/fertile — the adjective of productive richness) — used for the richest land and food; the same root as the anointing oil šemen. Maʿăḏannê-melek (royal delicacies, H4574 + H4428) — maʿăḏan denotes luxury food fit for a king; Asher's agricultural territory (Acco plain, Phoenician coast, Carmel region) would indeed produce the finest oil and grain. The blessing is entirely material — Asher's gift to Israel is its agricultural abundance.
21: naptālî ʾayyālāh šĕlûḥāh hannōtēn ʾimrê-šāper — "Naphtali is a doe set free that gives beautiful words." ʾAyyālāh šĕlûḥāh (H355 + H7971, a doe released/set free) — the graceful, swift deer freed from constraint. ʾImrê-šāper (beautiful words/sayings, H561 + H8228) — ʾimrāh (word/saying) + šāper (beautiful/pleasing). Whether "bears beautiful fawns" or "gives beautiful words," the tribe is characterized by freedom and graceful production. Deborah and Barak — from Naphtali's territory (Judges 4:6) — produce the great victory-song (Judg 5), which would fit "beautiful words."
22: ben pōrāt yôsēp ben pōrāt ʿălê-ʿāyin — "Joseph is a fruitful branch, a fruitful branch beside a spring." Ben pōrāt (son of fruitfulness — pōrāt from pārāh H6509, to be fruitful/bear fruit) — the fruitfulness verb from creation's mandate (Genesis 1:22,28: "be fruitful and multiply") and the covenant blessing. Joseph is the embodiment of covenant-fruitfulness — the one through whom the promise to Abraham ("I will make you fruitful," Genesis 17:6) is preserved in Egypt. ʿAlê-ʿāyin (beside a spring/fountain) — the spring imagery: fruitfulness requires a water source; Joseph's fruitfulness is rooted in proximity to a divine spring. Bānôt ṣāʿăḏāh ʿălê-šûr (his branches climb over the wall) — the vine-image of overflow: the fruitful vine exceeds its own boundary.
23: wayyĕmarărûhû ûḇallû wayyiśṭĕmûhû baʿălê ḥiṣṣîm — "With bitterness archers attacked him; they shot at him with hostility (wayyiśṭĕmûhû)." Mārār (H4843, to be bitter/shoot bitterly — Piel: to embitter/attack with bitterness) — the same root as "bitter" water (Genesis 15:23) and the bitterness of slavery (Exodus 1:14). Śāṭam (H7852, to bear a grudge/persecute/be hostile) — the same rare verb the brothers fear in Genesis 50:15 ("what if Joseph holds a grudge/śāṭam against us"). The verbal connection across the two chapters is deliberate: the brothers who shot at Joseph with hostility (śāṭam) fear that Joseph will now bear that same śāṭam against them — but Joseph's response in Genesis 50:19-20 dissolves the cycle.
24: wayyēšeḇ bĕʾêtān qaštô wayyāpōzzû zĕrōʿê yādāyw — "But his bow remained steady (bĕʾêtān), his strong arms stayed supple." ʾÊtān (H386, perennial/permanent/enduring — like an ever-flowing wadi, not seasonal) — the same word for the "ever-flowing stream" of Amos 5:24. Joseph's bow is ʾêtān: it does not waver or fail under pressure. Three divine titles follow: miyyĕdê ʾăḇîr yaʿăqōḇ (by the hands of the Mighty One of Jacob — H46 ʾăḇîr, the Bull/Mighty One, a divine title used in Psalms 132:2,5; Isaiah 49:26); miššâm rōʿeh (from there the Shepherd); ʾeḇen yiśrāʾēl (the Stone/Rock of Israel). The three names accumulate power and covenant-identity in a single verse.
25: mēʾēl ʾāḇîḵā wĕyaʿzĕreḵā wĕʾēt šadday ûyĕḇāreḵeḵā — "because of your father's God, who helps you, because of the Almighty (šadday) who blesses you." ʾĒl šadday — the patriarchal covenant name (Genesis 17:1; Genesis 28:3; Genesis 35:11; Genesis 43:14) invoked for Joseph's ultimate blessing. The blessings that follow catalog creation-provision: birḵōt šāmayim mēʿāl (blessings of the heavens above — rain), birḵōt tĕhôm rōḇeṣet taḥat (blessings of the deep lying below — springs and groundwater), birḵōt šādayim wārāḥam (blessings of breasts and womb — fertility of human and animal life). The tripartite structure covers the three vertical domains of creation: sky, earth, underearth — God's provision from all three. The word šādayim (breasts, H7699) may be the linguistic root of šadday — "God the nourisher" — though this etymology is contested.
26: birḵōt ʾāḇîḵā gāḇĕrû ʿal-birḵōt hōray ʿaḏ-taʾăwat gĕḇʿōt ʿôlām — "Your father's blessings are greater than the blessings of my ancestors, than the bounty of the age-old hills." Gāḇar (H1396, to be strong/prevail/be mighty over) — Joseph's blessings from Jacob gāḇar (prevail) over even the blessings of the patriarchs: they exceed what Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob received. Gĕḇʿōt ʿôlām (the everlasting hills, H1389 + H5769) — hills as symbols of permanence; the blessings match the permanence of geography. These will rest on Joseph's head (lĕrōʾš yôsēp ûlĕqoḏqōḏ nĕzîr ʾeḥāyw, on the crown of the set-apart one among his brothers).
27: binyāmîn zĕʾēḇ yiṭrāp babbōqer yōʾḵal ʿaḏ wĕlāʿereḇ yĕḥalleq šālāl — "Benjamin is a ravenous wolf; in the morning he devours the prey, in the evening he divides the plunder." Zĕʾēḇ (H2061, wolf) — the predatory wolf as tribal emblem; not necessarily negative (Numbers 23:24 uses lion-imagery for Israel's ferocity). The temporal structure — morning devours, evening divides — may trace the tribe's military history. Historical figures from Benjamin include Ehud the judge (Judges 3:15-30), King Saul (1 Samuel 9:1-2), and Paul the apostle (Philippians 3:5). Ṭārap (H2963, to rend as a wild animal) — the same verb Jacob used in 37:33 when he thought Joseph was torn (ṭārōp ṭōrap) by a wild animal; the wolf-verb connecting the two brothers across the narrative.
28: kāl-ʾēlleh šiḇṭê yiśrāʾēl šĕnêm ʿāśār ûzeh ʾăšer-dibbēr lāhem ʾăḇîhem wayyĕḇāreḵ ʾōtām ʾîš kĕḇirḵātô bēraḵ ʾōtām — "All these are the twelve tribes of Israel, and this is what their father said to them when he blessed them — blessing each one with his own appropriate blessing." Šiḇṭê yiśrāʾēl šĕnêm ʿāśār (twelve tribes of Israel) — the nation-in-embryo assembled before its founding patriarch. ʾÎš kĕḇirḵātô (each one with his own/appropriate blessing) — the prefix kĕ (according to/in conformity with) signals that each blessing is calibrated to each tribe's character and destiny; the blessings are not arbitrary but fitted.
29: wĕʾănî nĕʾĕsāp ʾel-ʿammî qiḇrû ʾōtî ʾel-ʾăḇōtay — "I am about to be gathered to my people. Bury me with my fathers." Nĕʾĕsāp ʾel-ʿammî (Niphal of ʾāsap, H622, to be gathered/added to) — "gathered to my people" is the standard death-formula for the patriarchs (Genesis 25:8,17; Genesis 35:29; Genesis 49:33), indicating not merely burial but reunion with the covenant-community in Sheol. The burial instruction for Machpelah is the third time Jacob requests it (Genesis 47:29-31; Genesis 49:29; Genesis 50:5,13) — the repetition signals the weight of the covenant-claim: Jacob insists his bones belong in Canaan, the promised land, with Abraham and Isaac.
30: bĕmĕʿārat śĕḏêh hammaḵpēlāh ʾăšer ʿal-pĕnê mamrēʾ bĕʾereṣ kĕnaʿan ʾăšer qānāh ʾaḇrāhām — "in the cave in the field of Machpelah, near Mamre in Canaan, which Abraham purchased." The full legal description — cave, field, location, purchaser, purchase party (ʾet-haśśāḏeh mēʾēt ʿeprōn haḥittî, from Ephron the Hittite) — echoes the legal precision of the original purchase narrative (Genesis 23). The repetition of the full title is covenant-documentation: Jacob is establishing that this burial place is legally owned Israelite land in the center of the promised inheritance.
31: šāmmāh qāḇĕrû ʾet-ʾaḇrāhām wĕʾēt śārāh ʾišeṭô šāmmāh qāḇĕrû ʾet-yiṣḥāq wĕʾēt riḇqāh ʾišeṭô wĕšāmmāh qāḇartî ʾet-lēʾāh — "There they buried Abraham and Sarah his wife; there they buried Isaac and Rebekah his wife; and there I buried Leah." The triple šāmmāh (there) marks the three patriarchal couples as joined by burial-place. Jacob includes Leah — not Rachel — as the covenant wife buried with the fathers; Rachel's grave is at Bethlehem (Genesis 35:19-20), outside the Machpelah community. The covenant line passes through Leah's burial: Judah, Levi, and the messianic lineage come from Leah.
32: miqnat haśśāḏeh wĕhamĕʿārāh ʾăšer-bô mēʾēt bĕnê-ḥet — "The field and the cave in it were purchased from the Hittites." Miqnat (purchase/acquisition, H4736, from qānāh H7069, to acquire/buy) — the legal term that concludes the property description; Jacob repeats the purchase-history to establish title against any future challenge. This phrase closes the legal cycle opened by Abraham's negotiation with the Hittites in Genesis 23 — the cave at Machpelah is Israel's first legally purchased property in the promised land.
33: wayyiggĕwâ wayyēʾāsep ʾel-ʿammāyw — "He breathed his last and was gathered to his people." Two death-verbs: gāwaʿ (H1478, to expire/breathe one's last/die — used specifically for patriarchal deaths: 25:8 Abraham, 25:17 Ishmael, 35:29 Isaac) + ʾāsap ʾel-ʿammāyw (gathered to his people — the covenant-reunion formula). The death of Jacob is the narrative close of Genesis; the book that began with creation ends with a patriarch's death and the covenant-community gathered in Egypt awaiting the promise of return. Wayyēʾāsep ʾel-ʿammāyw — "his people" are not merely biological kin but the covenant community of the dead — Abraham, Isaac, the patriarchs — to whom he is now joined.
